content:
{
  "title": "Cross-Lingual Scaffolded Prompting for Low-Resource Language Tasks",
  "problem": "LLMs struggle to perform well on multilingual tasks, especially for low-resource languages, due to limited exposure during pretraining and lack of annotated data.",
  "motivation": "Improving LLM performance on low-resource languages is crucial for global inclusivity and equitable access to language technology. Prompting is a low-cost intervention that can leverage pretrained knowledge without costly retraining. Advances in prompting strategies for cross-lingual contexts are timely as LLMs become more widely deployed.",
  "method": "We propose 'cross-lingual scaffolded prompting': for each low-resource language input, provide an in-context example in a high-resource language (e.g., English) paired with its translation, then supply the actual input in the low-resource language. The prompt format is: [EN example] ➔ [target language translation] ➔ [target language input] ➔ [model completes in target language]. This design aims to activate multilingual transfer and align task structure across languages, explicitly showing the model the mapping between high- and low-resource languages for the same task.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will test on tasks like sentiment classification and question answering in Swahili and Marathi using subsets of XNLI and TyDi QA. Baselines will include direct prompting and translation-augmented prompting. Metrics will be accuracy or F1 for each language. Improvements over baselines would indicate that scaffolded prompts boost low-resource performance."
}
